item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Many comments have expressed this, but I have to agree that I quite like the idea but not the execution.

+ Thoughts
- Thoughts

…In contrast to mundane water…

This would read a lot better as "In contrast to non-anomalous H2O".

…Testing has shown it capable of assimilating ice, steam, tea, fruit juice, seawater, blood, and [DATA EXPUNGED]…

Data expungements shouldn't often be used in places where I can't even fathom what could be underneath. What other substance could they be talking about, and why would they be hiding this information? Is it trying to hide another more secret anomalous liquid, and if so why not just say "…seawater, blood, and various anomalous liquids…"? It would work better for flow, be more intriguing, and not take me out of the article as much.

4. [DATA EXPUNGED]

This doesn't intrigue me, it just frustrates me. What does it do? Does it turn the human into an un-dead killing in constant agony? If so, tell us! It would be more interesting if you did.

There's a bunch of stuff like that around the article. Really possibly interesting concept, but I was too distracted to fully enjoy myself.

item.title: Enthalpy & Entropy
item.content:encoded:
I just noticed the article saying

in contrast to standard laws of enthalpy

in regards as to the ice's anomalous physical abilities. But Enthalpy refers to the energy of a substance and every substance tries to achieve the lowest energy level possible at a certain temperature, which the ice does (just at differen temperatures than water). Entropy on the other hand refers to the chaos of a substance: the higher the entropy the higher the chaos (gases have more entropy than solids). Every substance tries to achieve the highest entropy possible (which normally occurs at higher temperatures) , which also is inversed with the ice.

I'm not saying the article is wrong about this information, I'm just trying to show that enthalpy and entropy are closely linked as every substance tries to gain the highest possible entropy while also trying to get the lowest enthalpy possible at a certain temperature. Maybe one could re-write this part or could add the entropy to that.

Otherwise pretty neat little article and cool idea +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Exactly! I thought we were working to minimize all the censoring in old articles not put it back. This article has more black spots than a teenagers face…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Seconded.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Unpopular opinion: the rewrite only made things worse. It's now all [REDACTED] upon [DATA EXPUNGED] all the way through █████. I also believe O5 wouldn't use "SNAFU" in official documents.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You can have mine, I rarely vote anyway.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Noice, very noice.

Only thing I didn't like was the "would've" in "Had the site been closer to the water, there is little doubt an extinction-level event would've ensued." The contractions in the memo at the end are fine, but I don't like regular Addendums being that informal.

But otherwise, yeah, cool edit. Would +1 a second time if I could.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh, it seems we do. My mistake, apologies.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Don't we have permission with the current image?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: nvm

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So, this is probably a surprise, but I've recently remembered this existed and decided to clean it up a bit and address some of the issues. Ironically, I've never read that book. The Ice-9 thing was from a webcomic I used to read back in the day called 8-Bit Theatre.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
009, aka ice nine.
I can’t really rate this one, simply because I haven’t read cat’s cradle. I can’t determine if this scip is original or not. It’s a really interesting Idea, somewhat well executed, but the quality of it is heavily based on whether or not it’s original.
I do have to admit I take pleasure in all the people complaining about how this anomaly doesn't follow real world physics. Kinda the whole point.
Also all the people complaining about how it changes state at zero and -100, almost as if it was flipped in relation to water’s freezing temperature. I, too, find it unreasonable that water freezes at zero degrees. Almost as if Celsius revolves around it. Ugh.
Also, I agree that the situation in which they foind this should have led to the end of the world by now. Someone should really change that.
So yeah. If I ever read that book, I’ll come back and change my rating.
+0.

item.title: Re: physics dammit
item.content:encoded: It was explained in the report that the subject is potentially extra-dimensional in nature.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Because that's what the D-class are for. Besides, what date can we retrieve from mice that we can't get from humans?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I am something of a blizzard elemental. The ice and snow, the cold and wind speak volumes to in poetic form and prose.
As such… I do love this one. There aren't enough articles that cater to those tastes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: why test on D-class if you expected it to kill them, why not just use mice?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is basically Ice-9, but honestly less interesting because of the derivation. The whole kicker of this article basically relies on the reader not having read The Cat's Cradle or else the horrifying twist of what this stuff does when you touch it isn't a twist at all. That being said, it doesn't behave exactly like Ice-9, so I guess it is original enough. Changed my -1 to novote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Why it is subject?

Because this was written in 2009, when standards were lower and expectations for scientific wording weren't well-established.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why it is subject? It is object. It is not even alive or sentient.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"[No] less than 14 m3 … 3700 liters"

If there's 3700 liters then your container could be just 1.5 x 1.5 x 1.5 m in dimensions

item.title: Re: physics dammit
item.content:encoded:
It's more complicated than that, though- the shape and charge of molecules affect how they pack together and interact with more of themselves, and that can create strange phase-transition points where adding enthalpy to a material can cause it to freeze and removing it can cause it to vaporize.

We've also done some basic tinkering with exotic atoms that swap out parts of our familiar proton-neutron-electron matter with weird components like muons and pions. The resulting bizarro-world atoms seem to have odd properties for the brief time that they last, to say the least.

Basically, this stuff would be strange, yes, but not completely impossible.

item.title: physics dammit
item.content:encoded: this defies basicly every single one of them, where is the physics police to arrest this liquid or whatever it is. the more the particles in an object move around the hotter it is the less the move the colder it is, gas moves around alot, solids move alot less and this breaks those rules

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Staff notice: this user has been permanently banned for trolling.

item.title: Re: Spray this liquid at scp 682
item.content:encoded: They probably tried, but 682 used freeze breath. Or lowered it's body temperature.

item.title: Spray this liquid at scp 682
item.content:encoded: They should spray this liquid onto scp 682.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: After rereading this one, I disagree with the given classification.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I have some issues with using the terms "freezing" and "frost" in this particular SCP due to the temperatures and process that the SCP undergoes, but overall it is still a very interesting subject.

item.title: Image Attribution
item.content:encoded:
The 1st image in this article is a copyrighted image from http://www.gdargaud.net/Antarctica/WinterDC4.html and is used here with the permission of the copyright holder.


This automated message was sent on behalf of anqxyr. Please do not reply to this message or send other messages to this bot. Contact anqxyr in regard to this message. If you believe that you are not the intended recipient of this message, that the image was delivered incorrectly, or have other concerns about this bot, please contact anqxyr.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The image in this article was removed because we could not properly determine whether or not the image was properly licensed or attributed. It may be added once again if/when proper attribution or proper use under CC guidelines is confirmed. Please contact anqxyr or another member of the Image Team if you have questions.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Just a heads up, Devintrainer. Instead of replying to your own comments, you can edit your previous post using the "edit" function under the "options" tab to the lower right of every comment. That prevents spam buildup, and it's in the rules. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oops, did it as a reply to the above post.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, it's similar to Terraria's Crimson Biome while spreading through ice.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Interesting SCP. I was thinking that if a contaminated human were to live in very cold areas with this red water in liquid state under 0º, would he still survive in the cold? :P

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Maybe that's where SCP-500 comes from. Or maybe not. Write up your thoughts in a tale and we'll all find out!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What would happen if SCP-009 came in contact with SCP-006?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Remembering how my kool aid would freeze if I left it in the fridge too long. Red ice, heh. now I'm craving kool aid what the heckle

Its good work, tho. A little bland in parts, but nothing really downvote worthy tbh. As said by others, I think that Keter classification my be more fitting. It's really chilly ( laughs ) how quickly and effectively it could spread, since, you know, water is everywhere.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is one of those things we shouldn't be experimenting with more than necessary, although, yeah, the idea of an SCP-009-powered turbine that was mentioned a few pages back is worth exploring. Would also be interesting to know whether it evaporates or not.

As other people have already pointed out, though, this should /really/ be Keter (for the same reasons 008 is, if nothing else. We've found one instance of it. How do we know there aren't others hidden away somewhere in Alaska, or Northern Canada. Maybe Russia, even) the Bering Strait was right there, after all, although I don't have any idea how they'd have brought it across without accidentally killing everyone in the process.

EDIT: Oops; double hyphens trigger the strikethrough.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: And what if it went beyond that, into impossible negative temperatures?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So, If we heat this up enough, will it act as if it were at absolute zero?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As Light said waaaay back in 2009, this is like Ice-9. I like it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"At temperatures below -100°C, SCP-009 vaporizes into a gaseous phase similar to steam…"
yeah i like this one :)
+1

item.title: Staff Post
item.content:encoded:
Noting that a recent edit was reverted, as it was unnecessary. The edit in question changed the phrase "en masse" to "in masses".

"En masse", while arguably not exactly clinical tone, is not a misspelling and is a phrase used today to mean "in a group; all together".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was thinking of a no-vote for this until I got to this line:

If the subject comes in contact with any biological source of heat, it begins a runaway reaction in which the living organism's bodily fluids are rapidly converted to SCP-009 and promptly frozen by their own body heat (because of their generally high core temperatures, mammals are particularly susceptible).

Consider my bones chilled.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As other have mentioned, there are quite a few scientific inaccuracies. If you think about it, the line: "Because SCP-009 produces heat while freezing (at the same rate mundane ice consumes heat while melting)" is saying that SCP-009 behaves just like water, when it is obviously intended to be the opposite of water. Furthermore, since SCP-009 freezes in response to a temperature increase, if it produced heat at the same time it would be spontaneously generating heat, breaking conservation of energy. SCPs can break the laws of physics but that probably was not the intention. If this gets fixed, the line afterwards about a self-perpetuating reaction can be preserved by simply saying that the organism's body heat sustains the reaction, which seems to better match the fact stated earlier that an organism causes a runaway reaction more so than other situations.

Another inaccuracy is that evacuating the chamber, while intuitive, would be counterproductive. It is likely that "red ice" reacts the same way as water does to pressure, even though it reacts oppositely to temperature. In that case, evacuating the chamber would cause the SCP to rapidly boil and vaporize as it's molecules attempt to fill the vacuum, thus making it even harder to contain
.
Also feel like this could potentially be more fitting for keter-class. It requires a constant input of power to remain contained and any breach in containment could be catastrophic since it only needs to touch water vapor to spread. If god forbid a sample were to be dropped into the ocean or other large body of water the result would be straight-up apocalyptic.

Does this page currently have an "owner"/someone i can ask permission to edit? I'd like to at least change the exothermic/endothermic part.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I absolutely love the technicality of this, except it breaks a bit in the containment procedures when you mix up "subject" meaning the SCP, and "subject" meaning the Class Ds. I recommend using the term "specimen" because hooooly crap it's so clinical and scary.

I'd also avoid the term "victim" in the containment procedures, especially when the Foundation is doing the exposure to the Class Ds. But the use of "victims" in the addendum is definitely okay! They were just people who died horribly because of something beyond human understanding.

I only caught these because I was recording an audio version of this article.

item.title: pendantic, but
item.content:encoded: cold fusion means colder than hot fusion. It typically occurs in the 800 to 1000 degree Celsius range.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As well as the others have said the science dose need some changing. I dont know if this theory was proposed or not already but given that the ice was discovered as red with the "Victims" what if the Ice was red due to the blood freezing and the ice takes on the color, this being said if you were to introduce say greed colored water it would change to the color of the last liquid it absorbed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm still just a newbie here, but I think, as has been previously noted, that this SCP's real Achilles' heel is that it is logically impossible, given the conditions of its retrieval, that it would have vaporized from contact with the snow, entering the planetary water cycle and rapidly causing a major Keter-style event.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The word "victim" should be changed to "subject" in the article for three reasons.
1) Victim makes it sound like it was an accident that they touched the SCP.
2) Subject sounds much more scientific than victim.
3) The terms "subject" and "victim" are used close to each other so it kind of ruins the flow.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This was the SCP that made me fall in love with the site. It's simple and quirky. As I read it, my head slowly tilted to the side.

This should be the article you point to when directing someone new to the site. Point and say 'this, right here, is SCP in a nutshell.' And it only gets better.
Congrats, author.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Nearly all changes reverted. Yes nearly two and a half months later shush. Doesn't change the fact that that ain't how you go about making significant changes to an article.

Oh yeah, the article itself: I came back to reread 009, to see if my opinion of it changed. It didn't. The concept still bugs me way too much, and I can't ignore the scientific plotholes… but the article is almost written well enough for me to not care. Not enough to upvote, mind, but enough to not downvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Just been through and tidied up the article. It should read better now.

Also, I did remove the expungement as it seemed unnecessary. If anyone (especially the author) resents this - then I sincerely apologise in advance.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I certainly think this is a good article, although as Sli Fi has mentioned, the terminology does need clearing up.

Still though - upvoted.

EDIT- Can I have permission to correct/edit this article please?

EDIT- I will just make the terminology and technical changes as have already been suggested. As for the expungement…well I won't touch that, but it does seem unnecessary.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
With my background in thermochemistry, I really don't like how this article handles the SCP. The -100°C "boiling point" is extremely arbitrary, as discussed above.

There are also several mistakes in terminology. First of all, the term "aqueous solution" applies only to a homogeneous mixture in which water is the solvent. "Aqueous solution" could easily be replaced with "H2O" or something else that conveys the correct meaning. Secondly, the term "heat" is misused (the most blatant misuse is the phrase "consumes heat"). "Thermal energy" is a far more accurate term in several cases. Third, the melting of "mundane ice" is endothermic, and the freezing of "mundane ice" is exothermic. This article suggests that the same is true of SCP-009, rather than the inverse ("Because SCP-009 produces heat while freezing at the same rate mundane ice consumes heat while melting").

These mistakes shatter my suspension of disbelief, forcing me to downvote SCP-009.

item.title: Free power?
item.content:encoded:
If there was in a sealed chamber that had water and SCP-009 in it, but separated by a metal wall to transfer heat. Wouldn't both waters end up freezing, melting, and refreezing in an infinite cycle? Then you could add turbines to one or both sides of the container to produce a continuous supply of energy?

Maybe you could add an addendum about a generator like that?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't really like the way this entry is written, but the concept is so scary that I'm upvoting anyway.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Indeed. The conversion property means a single atom of this stuff would quickly convert all liquid in the Earth's atmosphere approximately as soon as it came into existence.

item.title: State change quibble
item.content:encoded:
I like this article, but there's a problem with the logic here that kind of took me out of it. "Because SCP-009 produces heat while freezing (at the same rate mundane ice consumes heat while melting)…"

Correct me if I'm wrong, but doesn't real world ice produce heat while freezing at the same rate that it consumes it while melting? You need to put heat into the system to melt ice, and take heat out of the system to freeze it. With the "inverted" red ice, you would have to take heat out of the system to melt it and put heat into the system to freeze it, meaning that SCP-009 consumes heat while freezing — it would suck the heat out of the unfortunate subject.

However, I don't think it would lower the subject's temperature enough to stop the freezing process — that wouldn't happen until 0 celsius, at which point the subject has been dead from hypothermia even if he/she somehow managed to survive the initial massive frostbite injuries. So making this change wouldn't require any substantial rewrites… the basic effect of the SCP is the same either way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I particularly like this one, but there is one little thing that pulls me out of immersion.

When the author mentions "altered by another reality in which the laws of physics are inverted." but only SOME of the laws of physics are inverted. If all were, we would have a substance that would be heated by the coolant and would scatter all over the place due to inverted interaction with gravity.

Upvoted, but would be a lot better immersion-wise if that phrase was changed to something like: "altered by another reality in which some of the laws of physics are inverted."
